title: Fluid
subject: physics
summary: Fluid mechanics in SPTG usually focuses on density, pressure, buoyancy, and floating conditions.
Harder questions may extend to pressure at depth, Pascal's principle, continuity, and basic Bernoulli ideas.
The key is understanding what quantity controls what: density affects weight per volume, pressure depends on force per area, and buoyancy depends on displaced fluid.
coreConcepts: Density is mass per unit volume.
Pressure is force per unit area.
In a static fluid, pressure increases with depth.
At the same depth in the same fluid, pressure is the same.
Buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.
An object floats when buoyant force equals its weight.
An object sinks when its weight is greater than the maximum buoyant force available.
Relative density compares density of a substance to that of water.
Continuity means incompressible fluid flow rate is conserved: narrower area leads to higher speed.
Bernoulli connects pressure, speed, and height along a streamline in ideal flow.
derive: Density is defined as mass divided by volume, so ρ = m/V.
Pressure is force per area, so P = F/A.
For a fluid column of height h and cross-sectional area A, the fluid weight is mg = ρVg = ρAhg.
Thus pressure due to the column is P = F/A = ρAhg / A = ρgh.
By Archimedes' principle, buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid, so Fb = ρfluid g Vdisplaced.
For floating equilibrium, upward buoyant force equals downward weight.
For incompressible flow, volume flow rate is conserved: A1v1 = A2v2.
Bernoulli in ideal fluid flow comes from energy conservation per unit volume: P + (1/2)ρv^2 + ρgh = constant.
formulas: Total pressure at depth: Ptotal = Patm + ρgh
Buoyant force: Fb = ρfluid g Vdisplaced
Relative density = density of substance / density of water
Pascal principle (same transmitted pressure): F1/A1 = F2/A2
traps: Pressure depends on depth, not on total volume of fluid in the container.
At the same depth in the same fluid, pressure is equal even if the container shape changes.
Buoyant force depends on displaced fluid, not on the object's entire volume unless fully submerged.
A floating object does not necessarily have zero buoyant force; it has buoyant force equal to its weight.
Density and relative density are not the same quantity.
Hydrostatic pressure ρgh is gauge pressure; total pressure also includes atmospheric pressure.
For continuity, smaller area means larger speed, not smaller speed.
Bernoulli is for ideal flow; do not use it blindly in every real-fluid situation.

sptgSet.question: Find the density of a substance of mass 20 kg and volume 4 m^3.
A force of 40 N acts on an area of 2 m^2. Find pressure.
Find the hydrostatic pressure at depth 5 m in water. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
A body of volume 0.02 m^3 is fully immersed in water. Find the buoyant force. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
An object has mass 4 kg and volume 0.01 m^3. Find its density.
Will the object in the previous question float in water? Explain using density.
sptgSet.answer: Yes, because 400 kg/m^3 is less than 1000 kg/m^3

sptgSet.question: A hydraulic lift has small piston area 0.01 m^2 and large piston area 0.2 m^2. If 50 N is applied on the small piston, find the output force.
Water flows in a pipe of area 4 m^2 at speed 3 m/s. It enters a narrower section of area 2 m^2. Find the new speed.
At what depth in water is the hydrostatic pressure 20000 Pa? Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
A floating block weighs 80 N. What is the buoyant force on it?

hardSet.question: A block of density 800 kg/m^3 floats in water. What fraction of its volume is submerged?
A solid of volume 0.05 m^3 and density 1200 kg/m^3 is fully submerged in water. Find its apparent weight. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
Compare the hydrostatic pressure at the same depth in two differently shaped containers filled with water.
A body displaces 0.03 m^3 of water while floating. Find its weight. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
A metal block of mass 10 kg and volume 0.001 m^3 is placed in water. Will it sink or float?
A hydraulic press has piston areas 5 cm^2 and 200 cm^2. If the input force is 25 N, find the output force.
Water enters a pipe section of area 0.04 m^2 at 2 m/s and leaves through area 0.01 m^2. Find exit speed.
Find the total pressure at depth 3 m in water if atmospheric pressure is 100000 Pa. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
An object weighs 120 N in air and 80 N when fully immersed in water. Find the buoyant force.
An object weighs 120 N in air and 80 N in water. Find the volume of displaced water. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.

pat3Set.question: A block of density 750 kg/m^3 floats in oil of density 900 kg/m^3. Find the fraction submerged.
A cube of side 0.2 m and density 500 kg/m^3 floats in water. Find the submerged height. Use ρwater = 1000 kg/m^3.
A container has water to depth 4 m. Find the hydrostatic force on a 0.5 m^2 horizontal surface at the bottom. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m^3 and g = 10 m/s^2.
Water flows through a pipe that narrows from 0.03 m^2 to 0.01 m^2. If the speed in the larger section is 4 m/s, find the speed in the smaller section.
In an ideal horizontal pipe, water speed increases from 2 m/s to 6 m/s. State how pressure changes according to Bernoulli.
A solid of density 2000 kg/m^3 and volume 0.01 m^3 is suspended fully in water. Find tension in the supporting string. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A floating object has 30% of its volume above water. Find its density. Use ρwater = 1000 kg/m^3.
A liquid has relative density 1.2. Find its density in kg/m^3.
A U-tube contains two immiscible liquids. One side has 10 cm of water. The other has oil of density 800 kg/m^3. Find oil height for equal pressure at same level.
A body experiences buoyant force 250 N in a liquid of density 1250 kg/m^3. Find displaced volume. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
